title: Academic foundations
dek: Eric C. Greene's path began with rigorous scientific training.

title: Biochemistry doctorate: telomere biology foundations
dek: Ph.D. in Biochemistry from Texas A&M University under Dorothy Shippen, focusing on telomere biology and establishing deep technical foundations.
narrative: Eric C. Greene's trajectory began with rigorous doctoral training in biochemistry at Texas A&M University. Working under Dorothy Shippen, a pioneer in telomere biology, he established the scientific foundations that would define his career.
This doctoral period built the technical depth that later enabled his groundbreaking work in single-molecule imaging and DNA repair mechanisms.
mapCaption: Ph.D. formation at Texas A&M University in Biochemistry under Dorothy Shippen.

title: NIH postdoc: advancing toward DNA recombination research
dek: Postdoctoral research with Kiyoshi Mizuuchi at the National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, Maryland.
narrative: The move to the National Institutes of Health marked a transition toward the DNA recombination and genome stability research that would become central to Greene's career.
Working with Kiyoshi Mizuuchi, he developed expertise that bridged molecular biology and biophysics, setting the stage for his innovative single-molecule approaches.
mapCaption: Postdoctoral research at NIH with Kiyoshi Mizuuchi.

title: Columbia University: building a research program in genome integrity
dek: Professor in the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biophysics at Columbia University, establishing the Greene Lab.
narrative: Joining Columbia University's Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biophysics, Eric C. Greene established a research program focused on genome integrity, DNA repair, and single-molecule biophysics.
His laboratory developed novel approaches to studying DNA-protein interactions, creating tools and techniques that would influence the broader field.
proofPoints: Pioneered the DNA curtains technique for high-throughput single-molecule imaging.
Published highly cited work on CRISPR-Cas9 DNA interrogation mechanisms.
Established research program studying RAD51 and homologous recombination.
mapCaption: Professor at Columbia University, New York.

evidenceProfile.strength: Primary university URLs support faculty position

title: DNA curtains: pioneering single-molecule visualization
dek: Development of the DNA curtains technique revolutionized single-molecule imaging of DNA-protein interactions.
narrative: The DNA curtains technique, first introduced by Eric C. Greene and colleagues at Columbia, combined supported lipid bilayers, nanofabricated diffusion barriers, and total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy to align DNA molecules for high-throughput single-molecule imaging.
This innovation enabled researchers to observe individual protein molecules interacting with DNA in real-time, revealing dynamics that bulk biochemistry could not capture.
proofPoints: DNA curtains technique widely adopted in single-molecule biophysics community.
Method enabled visualization of hundreds to thousands of DNA-binding proteins simultaneously.
Technique extended to single-stranded DNA, expanding experimental capabilities.
mapCaption: DNA curtains innovation at Columbia University.

evidenceProfile.strength: Multiple independent sources document the technique

title: CRISPR-Cas9 DNA interrogation: highly cited breakthrough
dek: Groundbreaking research on how CRISPR-Cas9 searches for and recognizes target DNA sequences.
narrative: Greene's 2014 publication on CRISPR-Cas9 DNA interrogation mechanisms became one of the most highly cited papers in the field, with over 1100 citations.
The work revealed how the Cas9 enzyme uses RNA-DNA base-pairing to locate target sequences, providing fundamental insights into CRISPR genome editing technology.
proofPoints: 1100+ citations demonstrate major impact on the field.
Research published during critical period of CRISPR technology development.
Work contributed to understanding of genome editing mechanisms.

evidenceProfile.strength: Direct publication record with citation metrics

title: RAD51 and homologous recombination: genome stability research
dek: Ongoing research program studying RAD51 recombinase family proteins and DNA repair mechanisms critical for genome integrity.
narrative: Greene's laboratory has made sustained contributions to understanding homologous recombination, the DNA repair pathway essential for maintaining genome stability.
Research on RAD51 and related proteins has revealed fundamental mechanisms of DNA repair with implications for cancer biology and genome integrity.
proofPoints: Multiple publications on RAD51 paralogs and recombination mechanisms.
Research connects to cancer biology through genome stability pathways.
Work spans from fundamental mechanisms to disease implications.
mapCaption: Ongoing DNA repair research at Columbia.

title: Cancer biology connections: genome integrity and disease
dek: Member of Herbert Irving Comprehensive Cancer Center, connecting DNA repair research to cancer biology.
narrative: As a member of Columbia's Herbert Irving Comprehensive Cancer Center, Greene's research on genome stability and DNA repair mechanisms connects directly to understanding cancer development and potential therapeutic approaches.
The work bridges fundamental biophysics with clinically relevant questions about how cells maintain genome integrity.
mapCaption: Cancer center research connections at Columbia.
